name: FinTech / Regulated
description: Conventions for compliance-aware teams building financial-services or other regulated software. Covers audit, encryption, retention, PII handling, and operational discipline expected by SOC 2 / ISO 27001 / GDPR auditors. Founder-curated — review before merging.

decisions.summary: Every state-changing operation writes an audit row with actor + before-state + after-state + reason
decisions.rationale: SOC 2 CC6.1 + 7.2 + 8.1 require attributable evidence of every change. The audit trail must include WHO did WHAT to WHICH entity WHEN and WHY. Reason is required even for routine operations — "normal_use" is acceptable; absence is not.

decisions.summary: Audit logs are append-only; no UPDATE or DELETE on `audit_entries` permitted at the application layer
decisions.rationale: An audit log that can be modified is not an audit log. DB-level guard (REVOKE UPDATE,DELETE) belt-and-braces with an application-layer rule. Compliance auditors check both.

decisions.summary: PII columns use deterministic encryption for equality lookups; non-equality fields use random encryption
decisions.rationale: Deterministic encryption preserves WHERE = lookups but allows frequency analysis. Random encryption thwarts frequency analysis but loses queryability. Use deterministic only when equality lookup is required — never default to it.

decisions.summary: Customer data retention is bounded by the active contract — auto-purge T+30 days after termination
decisions.rationale: GDPR Article 5(1)(e) (storage limitation). Indefinite retention is the default failure mode of relational systems — set explicit boundaries. T+30 days gives operations time to recover from a misclick before purge is irreversible.

decisions.summary: Backups are encrypted at rest with keys distinct from production-DB keys; rotation quarterly
decisions.rationale: A breach of the production environment must not auto-compromise backups. Distinct keys means an attacker who exfiltrates the DB key still cannot read the backup blob. Quarterly rotation bounds the blast radius of a leaked key.

decisions.summary: All access to production DB requires individual named credentials — no shared service accounts for human use
decisions.rationale: Shared service accounts make audit useless ("who ran this query?" → "the service"). Each engineer gets their own credentials with role-based grants. Shared service-account credentials are reserved for application code, not humans.

decisions.summary: Security incident response includes mandatory disclosure to legal within 24 hours of detection
decisions.rationale: GDPR breach notification window is 72 hours. Internal escalation to legal at 24 hours leaves a buffer to assess + draft + notify. Engineering does not unilaterally decide whether an incident requires regulator disclosure.

decisions.summary: Penetration testing every 12 months by an independent third party; findings tracked to closure in JIRA/Linear
decisions.rationale: SOC 2 CC4.1 expects independent testing at least annually. Findings without tracked remediation are findings, not closures — auditors check both the report and the remediation evidence.

decisions.summary: All third-party dependencies must clear an SCA scan in CI before merge; vuln severity ≥ high blocks
decisions.rationale: Software supply chain attacks are now a primary breach vector (cf SolarWinds, log4shell). SCA = Snyk / Dependabot / Socket / npm audit. High-severity vulns block merge; medium gets a 14-day SLA; low is best-effort.

decisions.summary: Production secrets live in a managed secret store (AWS Secrets Manager / Vault); never in env files committed to source
decisions.rationale: Source-committed secrets are a top-3 exfil vector (history is searchable, even after `git rm`). Managed secret stores provide rotation, audit, and access scoping. The `.env.local` file pattern is dev-only.

decisions.summary: Production database connections use mutual TLS (mTLS) — server cert + client cert both validated
decisions.rationale: TLS server-only authenticates the DB to the client. mTLS authenticates both directions, so a leaked client connection-string isn't enough — the attacker also needs the client cert. Higher operational cost; the right cost for regulated environments.

decisions.summary: All API requests require a request_id header; logged + traced through the full call chain
decisions.rationale: Distributed-system debugging is impossible without trace IDs. Request_id flows from client → API → background jobs → audit rows so an investigation can reconstruct a complete causal trace. Generate at the edge if the client didn't.

decisions.summary: Logs are scrubbed of PII at the boundary — never log full credit cards, SSNs, or email addresses
decisions.rationale: Logs are a high-volume PII-leak surface. The discipline: log identifiers (member_id, transaction_id), not values (email, card, SSN). A scrubbing middleware at the logger boundary catches drift; PRs reviewing log statements catches the rest.

decisions.summary: Every transaction over a defined threshold (e.g. $1k) requires a second-factor confirmation
decisions.rationale: Account takeover via password compromise is one of the highest-impact attack vectors in fintech. Per-transaction 2FA on high-value movement caps the damage window. Threshold is configurable per-customer based on risk profile.

decisions.summary: Anti-fraud rules run synchronously in-line with the transaction — never asynchronously after the fact
decisions.rationale: Async fraud detection means fraudulent transactions complete and the loss is sunk before detection. In-line means slightly higher transaction latency in exchange for a stop-the-bad-thing-before-it-happens guarantee. Worth it.

decisions.summary: All money values stored as integers in minor units (cents/pence) — never floating-point
decisions.rationale: Floating-point arithmetic introduces rounding errors that compound across millions of transactions. Integer minor-unit math is exact. Currency is tracked separately. Display formatting is the boundary that converts to user-visible formats.

decisions.summary: Idempotency keys are required on every write endpoint; duplicate keys return the original response
decisions.rationale: Network retries are inevitable. Without idempotency keys, retries can double-charge, double-credit, or double-execute. The pattern: client generates a UUID per logical operation, server stores `(idempotency_key, response)` for 24h, returns cached response on repeat.

decisions.summary: Database writes are wrapped in transactions; multi-table updates are atomic or roll back fully
decisions.rationale: Partial-write states are a debugger's nightmare. The discipline: any operation that touches more than one row in more than one table opens a transaction. Read-only operations need no transaction unless they require consistent snapshot semantics.

decisions.summary: Account access logs surface in a self-serve customer dashboard ("who logged into my account?")
decisions.rationale: Customers detect account compromise faster than the platform does in many cases. Surfacing access logs to the customer turns them into a partial security observer for their own account. GDPR also strengthens the customer's right to this information.

decisions.summary: GDPR data-subject-access-request (DSAR) responds in ≤ 30 days; export is machine-readable JSON
decisions.rationale: GDPR Article 15 + 20. The 30-day SLA is a hard regulatory deadline. JSON over PDF because (a) machine-readable per Article 20, (b) easier to generate from production systems, (c) easier for the user to import elsewhere.

decisions.summary: All deployments require a peer review approval; production hotfixes require post-merge review within 24h
decisions.rationale: Solo-merging to production is a single-engineer mistake-amplifier. Peer review catches a meaningful fraction of bugs. Hotfix exception exists because production-down beats process-purity, but the post-merge review is non-negotiable for the audit trail.

decisions.summary: Secret rotation cadence: API keys quarterly, DB passwords semi-annually, signing keys annually with overlap
decisions.rationale: Time-bounded secrets cap the blast radius of an undetected leak. Overlap windows (briefly accept old + new) prevent rotation from causing downtime. Audit logs record every rotation so post-incident analysis can pin a leak window.
